algorithms.name: Heap & Priority Queue
algorithms.analogy: An emergency room triage queue — the most critical patient always gets seen next, not the one who arrived first.
algorithms.story: You have 1 billion numbers and need the top 10 largest. Sorting takes O(n log n). With a min-heap of size 10: add each number; if it's larger than the smallest in the heap (the root), replace it. After all n numbers, the heap holds the top 10. Total: O(n log 10) = O(n). The heap is a ruthless meritocracy — only the best survive.
algorithms.whenToUse:
• Top-K largest or smallest elements from a stream
• Merge K sorted lists or arrays
• Find the Kth largest/smallest element
• Running median (two-heap pattern)
• Task scheduling by priority
• Dijkstra's algorithm (min-heap of distances)
algorithms.howItWorks:
Min-Heap property: parent ≤ both children (root = minimum)

Insert:
1. Add element at the end
2. Bubble up: swap with parent while element < parent

Extract-Min:
1. Remove root (minimum)
2. Move last element to root
3. Bubble down: swap with smaller child while element > child

Both operations: O(log n) — tree height
algorithms.interviewTips:
• Top-K largest → use MIN-heap of size K (counterintuitive — you remove the smallest to keep the K largest)
• Top-K smallest → use MAX-heap of size K
• Two-heap for running median: lower (max-heap) and upper (min-heap) differ in size by at most 1
• Java PriorityQueue is a min-heap by default; use Collections.reverseOrder() for max-heap
• Heap sort is O(n log n) in-place — heapify O(n), then n extract-mins each O(log n)
algorithms.useCases: [{"title":"Twitter Top Trending Topics","desc":"Track the top K trending hashtags from a stream of millions of tweets using a min-heap of size K — O(n log K) instead of O(n log n) sort."},{"title":"Process Scheduler","desc":"OS process schedulers use priority queues to always run the highest-priority ready process next."},{"title":"Prim's MST Algorithm","desc":"Prim's minimum spanning tree algorithm uses a min-heap to greedily select the cheapest edge connecting the next vertex."}]
algorithms.pitfalls: ["Using max-heap for top-K largest — you'd need to always remove, which is correct but confusing; min-heap of size K is the standard pattern","Not considering that Java PriorityQueue.remove(element) is O(n), not O(log n) — use lazy deletion or a TreeMap if you need arbitrary deletion","Heap with custom objects: must implement Comparable or provide a Comparator — forgetting this causes ClassCastException","Two-heap median: the rebalancing logic must guarantee size(lower) >= size(upper) to handle both odd and even stream lengths"]
algorithms.variants: [{"name":"Indexed Priority Queue","desc":"Supports decrease-key operation — allows Dijkstra to update priorities efficiently"},{"name":"Fibonacci Heap","desc":"O(1) amortized decrease-key — used in theoretical optimal Dijkstra, rarely in practice"}]
algorithms.mermaidDiagram:
graph TD
    A["Min-Heap\n        1"] --> B["3"]
    A --> C["2"]
    B --> D["7"]
    B --> E["4"]
    C --> F["5"]
    note1["Extract-min removes root(1)\nLast element(5) moves to root\nBubble down: 5→2→done"]
    note2["Insert(0): add to end\nBubble up: 0<4<3<1 → becomes root"]

algorithms.name: Monotonic Stack
algorithms.spaceComplexity: O(n) · worst case all elements in stack
algorithms.analogy: A queue at a concert where only taller people can see — when a tall person arrives, all shorter people in front are irrelevant (they can't see) and are popped off.
algorithms.story: Given heights [2, 1, 5, 6, 2, 3], find the largest rectangle. For each bar, you need the nearest shorter bar on both sides. Brute force: O(n²). Monotonic stack: maintain a stack where heights are always increasing. When a shorter bar arrives, pop — the popped bar knows its right boundary (current bar) and left boundary (new stack top).
algorithms.whenToUse:
• Next Greater Element (NGE) / Next Smaller Element
• Previous Greater Element / Previous Smaller Element
• Largest Rectangle in Histogram
• Maximum Area Rectangle in Binary Matrix
• Stock span problem
• Daily Temperatures (days until warmer)
algorithms.howItWorks:
Monotonic Increasing Stack (for Next Greater Element):
1. Initialize empty stack
2. For each element (index i):
3.   While stack not empty AND arr[stack.top()] < arr[i]:
4.     Pop index j from stack
5.     NGE[j] = arr[i]  (current element is next greater for j)
6.   Push i onto stack
7. Remaining elements in stack have no NGE (set to -1)
algorithms.interviewTips:
• Monotonic INCREASING stack → finds Next Greater Element (pop when you find a larger element)
• Monotonic DECREASING stack → finds Next Smaller Element (pop when you find a smaller element)
• Store INDICES in the stack, not values — you need indices to compute widths and distances
• Append a sentinel value (0 for histogram) to flush all remaining elements at the end
• Amortized O(n): each element is pushed and popped exactly once
algorithms.useCases: [{"title":"Stock Market Span","desc":"For each day, find how many consecutive previous days had a lower price — monotonic stack gives O(n) vs O(n²) brute force."},{"title":"Browser Back Button","desc":"Back button history is a stack — monotonic patterns apply to tracking the 'last meaningful page' before a series of redirects."},{"title":"Compiler Syntax Checking","desc":"Balanced parentheses validation is a classic stack problem — monotonic stack extends this to expression evaluation with precedence."}]
algorithms.pitfalls: ["Storing values instead of indices — you lose position information needed for width calculations","Wrong monotonic direction: increasing stack for NGE, decreasing stack for NSE — confusing these gives wrong results","Forgetting the sentinel flush — elements remaining in the stack after the loop have no NGE and must be set to -1","Histogram problem: width calculation when stack is empty after pop — width = i (from the beginning)"]
algorithms.variants: [{"name":"Monotonic Deque","desc":"Sliding window maximum in O(n) — maintain a deque of indices where values are decreasing; front is always the window max"},{"name":"Cartesian Tree","desc":"Build a tree where each node is the range minimum — equivalent to the monotonic stack process applied recursively"}]
algorithms.mermaidDiagram:
graph TD
    A["heights = [2,1,5,6,2,3]"] --> B["i=0: push 0 → stack=[0(h=2)]"]
    B --> C["i=1: h=1 < h[0]=2, push → stack=[0,1]"]
    C --> D["i=2: h=5 > h[1]=1\npop 1: width=1, area=1*1=1\npop 0: h=2>h[2]? No, stop\npush 2"]
    D --> E["i=3: h=6 > h[2]=5, push → stack=[2,3]"]
    E --> F["i=4: h=2 < h[3]=6\npop 3: area=6*1=6\npop 2: area=5*2=10 ← max!"]
    F --> G["Continue... maxArea=10"]
